summary: My name is Masao Hashimoto, born on June 29, 1900, in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. I came to America when I was 19 years old after jumping off a ship in Seattle because I wanted a better life. I worked on railroads in Montana and later moved to Seattle, where I learned English and became a Christian. Over the years, I worked various jobs including as a mechanic apprentice and a chauffeur, and I got married in the Congregational Church.

I was born in Shizuoka Prefecture, a farming village where most people, including my family, were farmers. The village had festivals that were fun, especially since I grew up in a poor family and enjoyed the chance to earn spending money. The area was about a mile from the sea and had hills far away. I attended school there for eight years and graduated junior high at age 15.

My family practiced the Zen sect of Buddhism, though we were not very devout. My father was a farmer and rather strict, while my mother was gentle. Although religion was part of our family life, I did not have any special memories related to it. Later in America, I moved away from Buddhism and became a Christian.

I arrived in Seattle at age 19 after jumping off a ship early in the morning around 4 a.m., wet and penniless. I stayed at a hotel where the owner was kind and helped me get dry clothes. I only stayed in Seattle for a couple of days before heading to Montana to work on the railroad. Later, in the summer of 1920, I returned to Seattle to work as a mechanic apprentice and attend night school. Seattle was where I learned English, became a Christian, and started my new life in America.

After arriving in America, I went to Montana to work on the railroad for about a year. I worked between Whitefish and Spokane, cleaning ashes from engines and turning them around in the roundhouse. It was hard work but I saved about $800 during that time. I learned English on the job and eventually became an oil boy because I could speak some English. Montana was my first experience working in America.

I worked on the Great Northern Railway in Montana, mainly cleaning ashes from the engines and turning them around in the roundhouse. The trains ran between Whitefish and Spokane, and my job was physically demanding but necessary. I also cleaned oil burners and extinguished fires on the engines. This was part of my first job in America after arriving in Seattle.

Along with the Great Northern Railway, I worked on the Northern Pacific Railway in Montana. My duties included cleaning ashes from the engines and turning them around in the roundhouse between Whitefish and Spokane. It was tough work but I learned a lot about the railroad operations. This job helped me save money and gain experience in America.

Whitefish was one of the towns where I worked on the railroad in Montana. Trains would come from Whitefish to Spokane and back, and my job was to clean the ashes and maintain the engines at the roundhouse in between. Whitefish was part of the route that defined my first year of work in America. It was a small but important place in my early immigrant experience.

Spokane was the other end of the railroad route where I worked in Montana. Trains came from Spokane to Whitefish and back, and I helped clean and maintain the engines during their stops. Spokane was part of the circuit that defined my first year of labor in America. It was a key location in my early work on the Great Northern and Northern Pacific Railways.

After moving to Seattle, I attended the Japanese and Chinese Mission located in the basement of the First Presbyterian Church. They offered free English classes three nights a week and Bible study on Sundays and Thursdays. We studied mainly the Old Testament, sang hymns, memorized verses, and prayed together. This mission played a big role in my learning English and deepening my Christian faith.

The First Presbyterian Church in Seattle housed the Japanese and Chinese Mission in its basement, where I attended English classes and Bible study. This church was important in my spiritual growth as it provided a place to learn about Christianity and practice worship. Later, with the help of Dr. Mark Matthews, the church helped establish Japanese and Chinese congregations. It was a central place in my transition from Buddhism to Christianity.

Dr. Mark Matthews was a famous pastor who helped build the Japanese and Chinese churches in Seattle. His efforts led to the start of Bible teaching at the night school of the Presbyterian Church where I studied. He played a key role in supporting the immigrant Christian community and strengthening the church's outreach. I benefited from the programs his leadership helped establish.

I worked as an unpaid mechanic apprentice at the Ikeda brothers' used car business in Seattle after leaving the railroad. The older Mr. Ikeda was the elder of the Japanese Congregational Church, and his younger brother was a deacon. I stayed at the Buddhist Church while working there and began attending the Congregational Church on Sundays. This job helped me transition into city life and deepen my Christian faith.

After starting work with the Ikeda brothers, I began attending the Japanese Congregational Church in Seattle. They held three services on Sundays: morning worship, Christian Endeavor in the afternoon, and evangelical meetings in the evening. I was baptized there by Rev. Abe on December 19, 1920, marking my formal commitment to Christianity. The church was central to my spiritual life and community involvement.

Rev. Abe was the pastor who baptized me on December 19, 1920, at the Japanese Congregational Church in Seattle. His role was important in my conversion and acceptance into the Christian faith. I remember the baptism as a significant moment in my life, marking my departure from my Buddhist upbringing. Rev. Abe's leadership helped guide me spiritually during my early years in America.
The transcript does not provide specific details about my relationship or experiences with the Furuya Company, so I cannot share much about it.
The transcript does not mention Three Tree Point, so I have no memories or experiences related to that place to share.
Tacoma, Washington, is not mentioned in my story, so I do not have any experiences or connections to that place to relate.
The Lincoln Motor Company does not appear in my narrative, so I have no direct experience or relationship with it.

I once worked as a chauffeur for a Jewish family whose employer sold Warner Brothers pictures in the Seattle area. The family liked Japanese people, and this job helped me improve my English and gain experience driving. It was part of my transition from unpaid mechanic work to paid employment. This connection to Warner Bros. was indirect but meaningful in my early American life.
New York City is not mentioned in my story, so I have no personal experiences or connections with that place to share.

Vancouver came up when the hotel owner told me he had no passport from Vancouver but came to Seattle with the help of an immigration officer. Also, around 1918, the yobiyose system from Vancouver was not allowed, which affected my plans to be summoned by relatives. Although I never lived there, Vancouver was part of the immigration context I experienced when coming to America.
